children.text: Again, people who are interested in disabilities, who are interested in homelessness, who are interested in stereotyping the black stereotype, taking it ... Very strange piece. What's so interesting is that the Berlin audience, they're so sophisticated. They're sitting there, quiet. I'm looking around to see what they're thinking. At the end, they're all on their feet. Standing ovations.
Wow.
It was like, did they understand what they saw? Yeah. It's an amazing city.
Well, I've heard that Berlin has had its moments of real artistic ...
Yeah.
That's where people were going.
Yeah.
They were leaving the States and going to Berlin.
Right, and certainly even before the Nazi regime, that was the first place they were going. Yeah.
It's such an interesting ...
Yeah.

That came up with Jaamil and I's conversations, as we talk about America. Like you said, it's so steeped in the black and whiteness. [crosstalk 00:01:14] you're able to begin to make work that isn't colored.
Yeah, exactly. Right. [crosstalk 00:01:27]
By just the fact of blackness.

Right. The fact of blackness, that's a good term. Yeah. When we're talking about this, I just thought, wouldn't it be marvelous if you got this all funded and did this, and then after this, you went to Tricia Brown, Twyla Tharp, to these people and said, choreograph something on me, for me, or one of the things that you did? Just in terms of this, well, let's switch it around.
Mm-hmm (affirmative). Yeah. Okay. One of the things I was thinking about was how this ... I was like, flip the lens.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).

Yeah. It was in relationship to this. We started having that conversation about understanding the black body through appropriation. What happens when you switch it, and you understand ... It's not understand the white body, but that you ... Somehow, it doesn't go backwards. You flip it. Like what you're saying, I do Twyla's work from such and such a time, and obviously, you see it.
Uh-huh.
Instead of going, I've worked with black dancers.
Yeah.
It's this way.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
How has African aesthetics been influenced ... That becomes delensed, to understand this other work.
Right.
It's probably happening 10 more years from now. We'll be reading hip hop like that.
Yes, exactly.
It will be on the white bodies.
Yeah, already. [crosstalk 00:03:34]
That becomes this other thing.
Yeah, already.
-said this great thing about jazz. He's like, most of the stuff that you're seeing in the studio, that's jazz. That's no hip hop.
Okay, interesting.
It used to be that jazz had this very Europoeanist ...
Mm-hmm (affirmative). That's right.
You still have that.
Yeah.
We keep changing the name of whatever is current.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
For a moment, jazz was it, current, modern.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
That kept changing, so now, jazz has [crosstalk 00:04:11]
Yes. Right.
-hip hop flavors.
Right, yeah.
Gestures.
Yeah or more Africanist, I would say.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
Yeah.
But we don't call it jazz anymore.
Uh-huh.
It's almost like this black dance term.
Right.
Jazz is old.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
At the time, jazz was new. Then we had modern. Now, modern is old.
Yeah.
Now we have contemporary, post modern.
Yeah.
It just keeps-

All of them, of course have Africanist resonances, and always have. If you think about the formality of Matt Mattox, and I loved that stuff ... He used to teach in New York, before he moved to London or something, when I had just gotten out of college in the early ... When did I graduate? 1963. So 1964, 1965, he was teaching on 14th Street. I loved his class and these things. He would say, you know what this is about. Well, this is like rolling the dice. I was like, oh great.

Of course, all of that changed. Any of that is coming from a different aesthetic, even the formality of it. He was not a guy who was about 12 o'clock extensions or anything. He worked very low, and maybe that's the thing the jazz differentiated itself from Eurocentric folk dance, or Eurocentric classic ballet, was that it was get down stuff. You started from a low position. Yeah.
It's so funny. I think a lot of these artists were not necessarily so interested in the 12 o'clock.
Right.
It's added as you start to make things more ...
Yeah.
I don't know. Would the word linear ...
Balletic.
Balletic.
Yeah.
The line somehow gets added to what social forms were, these structures or whatever.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
I don't know if you want this, but I get hit if I do this.
Yeah. [crosstalk 00:06:52] When you lean forward, it's bad. When you're leaning back, it's great.
Oh, you like that?
Yeah.
Oh, okay [crosstalk 00:06:59]
Stay back.
I'll scooch you back just a little bit. That's okay.
I saw it, yeah.
Yeah. Now I can see.
Is it too much for you?
It's a little much, yeah. I don't know it could be angled [crosstalk 00:07:13]
Let's see. [crosstalk 00:07:13]
I can deal with it. I'll just try not to look at that.
How's that? Is that better?
That's fine, but is that doing what you need? I'm kind of leaning.
She looks like she's in the dark.
Yeah, when you-
Go ahead and put it where you need it to be. That's fine.
Are you sure?
Yeah.
She's tough. You know that.
I can take it.
Not only is it giving you nice face light, it's giving you a little eye sparkle.
I'll take sparkle. I'll take that.
It makes you look intelligent. I don't know if that helps at all. I could also ...
We didn't get to have this conversation, but you wanted to talk about Jawole's piece.

I thought that was what you wanted me to talk about. I read everything that you gave me. I watched the earlier stuff several times. In the treatment, it had said, Brenda then talks about Jawole's influences with Africanist aesthetic. I took it very seriously. I did my homework.
Well, I'll have you talk about it. You did your homework. [crosstalk 00:08:30]
We could just talk this way, if you want.
Yeah.

We can talk freely. I had also pulled out things from the Black Dancing Body, from the interview. I love the interviews in here. They're wonderful, the interviews with Jawole, about things. That point, where I had asked everybody, what do you think when I say the term black dance? She said a really nice ... We don't have to do [inaudible 00:09:00]. We can do whatever.
Jawole is a part of the project.

Right, yeah. It sounded so authentic and such a lovely, overarching definition. I say, what images come to mind, when I say black dance? Jawole says, a rainbow. I see a rainbow, because I think black dance is really like who we are in society. We are absolutely everything and everyone, and as the black experience, it means that you can be culturally white, raised in the middle-class white suburbs, and that is your orientation, or it could mean that you were raised in the inner city, but you love classical music and the opera. To me, it absolutely is, it really is, the full spectrum of, I would say, the American experience. I feel like that's so right. In a sense, that maybe debunks the idea of talking about black dance, because what we're saying is, as I try to say in the Black Dancing Body, that it infuses, permeates, every kind of movement that we do as Americans, black or white. That was certainly the point of digging the Africanist presence. It's everywhere. It's in ballet and stuff.
I felt like that was so super important. I think, in some ways, that's what's interesting about the question and this project.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
It's wanting to acknowledge that, but also knowing we're able to do this work because you and other scholars have done the work to talk about the omission.
Yes. Right.
Now that work is underneath that. How do we understand this question of black dance? It's so funny that she said rainbow, because it's one of my quotes. I don't know if you know this.
Oh, right.
I don't know what I said. We're as diverse as the colors in a rainbow.
Okay.
It's so great. I use it in a slightly different way, but it was a rainbow.
Yeah.
Things get so black and white.
Exactly.
You don't think of rainbow.
Right.
As a descriptor.
Right.

I wonder about this question, about black dance. It is American dance. Where does a project like this fit in that conversation, or how is it in conversation with the fact that black dance is part of the American experience?

Because it's invisibilized. It seems to me that what you're doing is, and of course we have to acknowledge that most of America, black or white, doesn't know a lot of other choreographers ... To say that black concert dance choreographers are invisibilized is that much further away, because we know white choreographers are not known by most. Everybody does think of So You Think You Can Dance.

Certainly, even more, the choreographers that you are bringing to the fore are invisibilized. Because they deal with the texture and feeling of being African American in society, which Jawole said also, which they all say, David RoussÃ¨v, Reggie, everybody, that, because you're dealing with that, you are bringing to the fore an American experience that happens to be, if you will, a black experience. It's something that Americans need to know about. Just as I said, Americans need to read Ta-Nehisi Coates. Probably, most people reading Ta-Nehisi are black. If not, then they are the intelligentsia.

Same with this poet, Claudia Rankine, who just won some big poetry award, and she's on the American Poetry Council. That book Citizen, where you see these incredible poems that are ... They're paragraph poems that are basically around the kinds of incidents that she's encountered, being an African American scholar, and living in a white world, what it means to have to function and deal with a constant sense of racism. Well, the worst kind is, of course, the implicit bias and microaggressions, that you experience being in a white space. She's a professor at some white university, et cetera. She basically lives in a white space.

This book is just incredible. This kind of thing, people talk about a lot. She made a phone appointment with a therapist, I think a trauma therapist. She doesn't explain why. She's made the phone appointment. She gets to the woman's house. The entrance is on the side door. She goes to the side door. She opens the gate. Somebody comes out and says, what are you doing here? Get away. You're not supposed to be here. She says, I made an appointment with you. The woman says, oh yes, you did.

The book is full of these kinds of ... I call them anecdotes, but they're poems. She's well known. This is like her fifth, I think, volume of poetry. It's devastating. Again, why you are doing this work is because of the way in which a Claudia Rankine is invisibilized in the white world, the way these choreographers are invisibilized in the white world.

I think you're taking it the next step, after the five traditional African American dance companies, Dallas Black, Cleo Parker Robinson, DCDC, Lula Washington and Philadanco. Then, of course, there's Ailey. Most people know some of the Ailey works, but those have been the only repositories for unbelievably rich and endearing, and spiritually and intellectually fulfilling choreography, by black choreographers.

Christopher Huggins is not known anyplace else, although he does get work in Europe. You don't see The Pennsylvania Ballet commissioning a work by Christopher Huggins, and they certainly could. Those traditional companies have taken up the torch, to preserve on the black dancing bodies of their company members, this living black archive. Those companies, though, Christopher Huggins ... Name some of the other choreographers. I'm blanking right now. Jawole has done ...
Jawole.
Yeah.
Bebe Miller.
Yeah, a little bit of Bebe. Just a little, yeah.
They don't have a lot of Alonzo King either.

No, they don't yeah. Anyway, just to say that you, then, are taking it ... What this project's significance is, is that you are taking it the next step. Certainly, you're not seeing a David RoussÃ¨ve work, or an Andrea Woods work, in that kind of black dancing body archive. All of these archives are necessary.
I'm thinking about the new ones. There's a moment, again, this shift, as I was making the work, that was particularly true when I was coming up.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
I wasn't seeing Bebe and David RoussÃ¨ve.
Yeah.
On those companies.
Right.
Now, I think Cleo has a David RoussÃ¨ve piece.
Oh, okay.
They have two. They just brought one back not that long ago.
Okay.
They became a little more established.
Uh-huh.
Then you're like, okay, we'll try it.
Yes.
The dancers became more versatile too.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
Now, in training, everybody's learning Contact and Proposition-
Exactly.
And the codified techniques are falling by the wayside.
Yes.
People aren't learning Horton and Graham.
Yeah.
In Julliard, yeah, but in your general modern dance departments, it's the sort of generic [crosstalk 00:19:15]
Right. What is modern dance, at this moment?
Exactly.
Yeah.
I'm curious how that conversation is changing. We've been talking about succession and legacy. This is the next step in that archive.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
I'm wondering about the shift in language, the shift in aesthetic, the shift in ... What becomes of those companies, that archive, if that starts to feel like that's ...
Old school?
Yeah. If that starts to just be seen ... I think it's something IABD struggles with.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
If this seems to be an old school question of archiving black dance.
Yeah.
Maybe you can speak, also, to how this project is trying to grapple with that question. How are we still engaging with this idea of black dance, or not, or American dance?
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
Is that conversation still relevant? I like what we ended up talking about, as a group, too. Is there a way that it ends up taking up all this space in the room?

Right, yeah. I'm more interested in talking about African American choreographers, African American dance companies. Again, I'm using that word, African American, because I feel like that descriptor is more appropriate. I'm also interested in the debt that is owed to African American dancers and choreographers, that has yet, like many debts that are owed to us, to be paid.

The one thing to be said about those five traditional dance companies, and the Ailey company, is, as you said, they are taking in and embracing the choreographers who had been new, but who now are more established. David RoussÃ¨ve has been around for, what, 30 years or something. Now that he has established himself, eh can be taken into the fold, if you will.

They are indeed, I think, consciously attempting, as much as their mission allows, to stay on the cutting edge. Rennie Harris has choreographed for, I think, all of those companies and for Ailey, which is just incredible. Rennie, who I believe was one of the first people to bring hip hop dance to the concert stage, he too now is in his fifties. He's been choreographing for something like 4 decades, since he was a teenager.

There is movement in those companies. They don't just become stagnant or an old horse. What makes me so sad is how they all struggle for survival. Again, Joan Myers Brown always says this. She has trouble even getting supporters of color, who are more likely, if they're well off in Philly or New York. In New York, they'll support Ailey, even if they're from Philly. In Philly, they would be more interested in supporting the orchestra or the ballet. There's a certain segment of the successful, professional African American population for whom it is to their advantage to be seen in the white space.
That is so sad. That is what has undermined us, and undermined our social ascendancy, for so long. You're trying to get your foot in the door.
Hmm. I feel like dance has a particular struggle too.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).

Maybe because it deals with body, and also because black dancing bodies ... The social form and going up against puritanical, you're not supposed to do that with your hips, and you're not supposed to that with your groundedness. We're interested in ascension.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).

I wonder if you can speak to the importance and the immediacy of the black dancing body, why that is so important, and maybe such an important lens to be asking some of these questions about race, and the way that we understand our American fabric.

Yeah. Of course, since the beginning of our American culture, the black dancing body has been an equal partner in doing the work here. Not that we were dancing in the sugar cane or the cotton fields, but those were our bodies. Afterward, or in between, we did find some ways to celebrate. Don't ask me how. Again, all of the squares, like Congo Square, et cetera.

Black dancing body principles have been with us for 400 years, ever since blacks and whites clashed in the Americas. Of course, there's been the issue of appropriation, if not just outright ripoff, from those beginnings. Also, of seeing the black dancing body as a performative trope. This is what they do, and this is what they do well. They can also work like animals. They are the ones who can move their hips or articulate their torsos, and we can just watch.

Then, of course, in the case of white minstrelsy, supposedly it began in the 1820's or something. Thomas Daddy Rice, who supposedly sees this crippled old black man. He's a vaudeville performer. Well, they didn't call it vaudeville them. He was a popular entertainer, performer. He sees an African American who is maybe homeless or whatever, 1820's. He's doing some strange little dance outside the theater, to gain alms. It has a little rhyme to it. [inaudible 00:27:12] jump Jim Crow.

It's like the story that Alberta Hunter tells, about Sophie Tucker, saying, oh, come into my dressing room and sing some of your songs for me. Thomas Daddy Rice indeed ... However the story goes. There are many versions. He either actually takes this man's clothes, gets to learn the dance and gives him some clothes. That's the kind of first formal piece of appropriation of the black dancing body, 1820's. This is the beginning of that whole principle of minstrelsy, which od course began with whites. Daddy Rice also goes as far as painting up his face, to look like this person.

Again, all of the ways in which black dancing bodies were put to the point of ludicrousy, and exploited as the new, and, if you will, that period's avant garde of performance, which then became a big hit of course. When black minstrels come in, they have to do the same thing, of blacking up, as well. That's just ... Your question. This black dancing body thing and the issue of who it is, and how it's being used, and the relationship between appropriation, challenge, competition, adoration, dissimulation, love hate relation, the exotic erotic. We were certainly always very exotic, and we were also their erotic, on and off the stage, in and out of their beds.

That trope of the entirety and the continuity of black dancing bodies being the model and the desired of the white population, has a long history. It's been talked about in various sundry ways. There are great books out, for example, on minstrelsy. Again, we talk about it in our separate boxes. If you were to go to, I don't know, 42 Street ... No, maybe not 42nd Street. If you were to go to a movie theater or someplace, the kinds of things that people are seeing in films now.

Film still seems to be, besides television and the internet, a really viable form of American education. They aren't dealing with any of this. Even recent history, Selma. I'm kind of extending what you're talking about with the black dancing body, and just saying that the black body, in the dance of life, if you will ... That movie did not get nominated for one Oscar. Are you kidding me? Of course, I know there were other movies, Twelve Years a Slave. I don't know if that was nominated for things. Again, when it is a black person who is at the helm, and a black female, Ava DuVernay, at the helm, tell the story, there's always some white pushback. Oh, she didn't talk about LBJ the way ... She demonized him. Please.
I know I got way off.
Maybe it's why dancers and choreographers ... I think we look at time, space, dynamics, body in theses places.
Mm-hmm (affirmative). Right.
I think we are, in some ways ...Like a dance studies and what dance studies can do.
Yeah.
It's uniquely situated in a way to notice the way bodies affect space, and are affected by space, and how they interrupt space and disrupt space.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
And revitalize space and reclaim space.
Right.
We look at primarily on the concert stage, but it's a larger conversation. We're uniquely situated with the focus that we have, on the bodies.
Yes.
To analyze and notice what the bodies are doing.
Right.

I wonder, if we look at the bodies, within this project, what are some of these ... Maybe this is a simpler question. What are some of the things that you were struck by, just by watching ... You've seen the whole project now. That's on video.
Yeah.
I'm curious, what were some of the things that are resonating with you after seeing these performances?

Yeah. I think Jaamil put it very well when he congratulated you last night, on just the curation of the first evening. Your two solos had, interspersed between them, works by Rennie and Cleo, which gave a nice kind of ... Not necessarily historical, but a nice kind of diverse perspective on a certain legacy of black dance and black dancing.

I was struck, as I said, by the fact that Diane choreographed something new on you. I think that was wonderful, that she took that opportunity to place her work on this relatively young, new body, that had experienced lots of other things through your life, in this time, as opposed to hers. It was humorous. I loved that. I loved the music choices, the blues, going back to that, and the mud, all of that kind of thing.

I love the blues. It's just amazing to me that most of the people who make the blues now are white, and most of the people who are going to blues clubs. All of these forms still stay alive, even though there is hip hop. There is still jazz. There is still blues. I loved that.

Of course, not to keep raving about you, but your performance was exquisite. It was really beautiful. The David RoussÃ¨ve work, it's so nice to have heard his words before we saw this very raw piece. You may remember that I saw the program in Philadelphia some years ago, so I do remember it from then. I think he had choreographed it right after the second Bush. It really resonated a lot then, around the marriage thing, as well.

Again, the immediacy of the issue, that it's still there, and the incredible high effect juxtaposition of having Nat King Cole just croon to us. If yesterday could only be tomorrow. You see this woman, totally the antithesis of that, going through this excruciating experience. I don't know what else to say about performances lats night.

Again, I was looking for these points of contact with Africanist aesthetics. Of course, one of the things about David's choreography and Diane's is that they, at least Diane, definitely grew up studying, even if it were at Karamu. She was studying white dance forms, modern dance, Anna Sokolow, et cetera. But there is that legacy, that line of continuity, that runs through it, because of the choices that she takes and makes in her own choreography after that. For her to always use live musicians, I think that's so incredible. What she said on camera in New York. I would go and sit on the side, and just listen to the musicians. That's so Africanist.

In African traditional societies, in some of them, there's not a word for dance. It's the whole thing. It's the dance. It's the music. It's the ceremony. It's the event. They are all part of it. The fact that's he starts from the music point and allows that to inform how she enters the circle, if you will, is, again, very Africanist aesthetic perspective.
What else? Ask me something about last night. Yeah.
Rennie's work.

Yeah, okay. I didn't know if I should speak on other people's works. Yeah. I am so sorry that ... AM I going to see the beautiful work that he choreographed for you and Mecca? Okay. That is superb, I thought. I'm a real admirer of Rennie's work. This was definitely a work in progress, that we saw last night. It's great. What you have to do, of course, as a choreographer, is put it out there as a work in progress, in order to know what it looks like, how it feels, how the performers are relating to it, and where it ultimately needs to go. That was interesting.

To talk about Cleo, it was so wonderful. I think I said this to several people last night. To see Cleo's work in the context of a concert of other people's work ... This whole thing of choreographing to songs. That, again, is such an African American thing to do, to choreograph to song. Of course, in traditional African cultures, you always have a song that goes along with everything you do. There are work songs. There are birth songs. There are all kinds of songs. These were like our African love songs, if you will. I don't know about everybody in the audience, but everybody of a certain age certainly knows ... Everything must change.

It brings up, it calls forth, all of these other associations that you bring to this beautiful dancing body, that you see in front of you. This whole thing that frequently is done in African American forms, which is also done in ballet, and I would say not so much in African forms, but this whole thing of reaching beyond the body, reaching beyond your kinosphere. Also, there's a place, reaching beyond. That movement and that looking stands for almost our whole lives, that we've been lusting after that place, that is some place for us.

That's what happens when you go to a dance concert, especially a good dance concert. It brings up all kinds of other associations. It's what you see on the stage, but also what you bring from your experience, to amplify and encircle, and embrace, the dancing bodies that are there. As I said last night, this whole thing of high effect juxtaposition, that you do something that's very calm, and then you ... The power of that kind of break. It's called high effect juxtaposition, but it's also about a break. Not just the break in the music or whatever, but break in movement as well.

All of those kinds of thoughts were roiling through my brain. Yeah. Also, I was very aware, especially when I used to write criticism, which I hated, because I certainly didn't want to critique things ... I love writing feature articles, but I don't think people should be criticizing art. Feeling the audience, and feeling what people are going through around me. This you woman, who did get up and speak at the end, because there wasn't much time for conversation, she had a scarf on and she thanked you for the performance, she was weeping beside me. This was with all of those things. I just put my hand on her knee.

I'm sure you felt this vibe from the audience. It was a kind of optimal environment for this performance, the whole evening, the whole event. It's optimal, always, as we both know, for a performance to feel that what you're giving is being received. That whole idea of call and response. Last night, I really felt the call and response was operative. Okay. Yeah.
It's actually been really nice to ... You do the work, and then ... I'm glad that people are getting so much out of it.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
That same woman that came up and spoke to me, she has cancer, and that's why she has the-
Oh, that's why she has a scarf on. Wow. Is she a student? No.
She may have been.
Okay.
I asked her, because she was an usher, I was like, are you in the department? She said something like, I used to be, or I'm going to be. That's when she said she had cancer.
Oh wow, bless her.
I don't know if she was a dancer. There's something about her build that sort of reads as dancer to me.
Yeah.
She was like, I'm going to be back.
Yeah.
Her name is Harmony.
Wow. I like that.
Even in the conversations we had, like with the young woman trembling, I just don't know how to say this thing, but I know that I'm alive right now on the inside.
Yes.
I'm not black.
Yeah.
That's the thing about this work and the undoing racism work.
Yes.
It's so important, because what this race thing does is strip us all of our humanity.
Exactly [crosstalk 00:45:05]

You have this person who's like, I don't know how to say these words, but I'm feeling a lot. The difference between, I now need to take care of you and your insecurity, to have this feeling of a sincere, I want to know ...
Yes.
I am alive by this. Something someone said to me, and I think this is underneath all of it to me, she was like, I left the concert wanting to dance.
Ah, lovely.
I wanted to go to the studio.
Uh-huh.
I'm like, yeah.
Wow, yes.
If this portal of these artists ...
Right.
The permission, perhaps, that is given to you, to tap into music, to tap into your passion, to tap into emotion ...
To go deep.
To go deep.
Yes, exactly.
You have these choreographers who are not afraid.
Yeah.
Even if it's artist who are considered to be very abstract, like Bebe Miller or Ralph Lemon or whatever, still this vitality in the work ...
Exactly, yeah.
If you feel like this gives you the permission to go deep-

Yeah. I loved the graduate student, Erica's student, Michelle. I couldn't believe it. She said, tonight, for the first time, I was proud of my school. I love that she didn't say, proud of my university. It sounded like, I'm back in high school. That kind of naÃ¯ve honesty about it, I'm proud of my school for the first time. That's so interesting, because we don't talk about that kind of stuff in graduate school. We don't talk about a pride in the effort to reach body, mind, spirit, as a totality, rather than an intellectual approach that's going to get you your MFA.

That's what she was talking about. She felt proud of that connection. It was like the other young woman, that she felt alive. She felt proud that she had been given ... Then, where does she go from there? Again, neither you nor I can ... Well, you have a way of at least mentoring that, because you teach here, but that is really for them to find. What is it that you do, then? You don't try to hold onto something, because that will blow it. What do you do, then, when you are gifted this kind of knowledge in a performance? Where and how are you able to keep it alive, and to keep it growing?

It does have something to do with what you said at the door. How you enter the studio, and I hope I indicated that last night, and how you leave. The whole thing, another Robert Farris Thompson canon, about exits and entrances. He talks about that, how you enter the circle, for example, and exit it. How you enter the space of your class, even if it's a sit down history class, how you are present in that moment, how you leave that, and then how you use what happened in that time.

I think people really experienced being present. It's so interesting, because there are so many distractions in our culture, that allow you to not feel that. It was so good that it wasn't a regular Q&A after. That just moves you away from the performance. The only way to stay present with the performance is to find another way to talk about the performance. I think that was happening. That's what allowed them to stay present with it, even through the conversation.

It was exciting to feel that, even this week, a type of aliveness. I think some of it comes down to just really being true. I feel like a lot of these dancers are on fire, and they don't want to separate themselves from what they're feeling.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
They're trying to actually figure out how to tap in and speak in a way, but still be ... TO do it critically, to do it with a level of sophistication.
Yes.
Give me these tools, but I want to use them to understand how to access my voice.
Yes.
I feel this desire. It was exciting for Cleo to come and tech class. They just ate it up.
Uh-huh.
There are times when I feel like people can be like, oh my god. They were like, yes.
Okay. How wonderful. [crosstalk 00:50:44]
I was excited that they got into it.
Yes.
Instead of feeling ...

Mm-hmm (affirmative). Yeah. Again, I don't know about the history of this dance department, and of course Onye Ozuzu was here, so there was certainly an Africanist legacy to be built upon in this department, but it sounds as though this is a kind of experience that ... Again, as you said, some of your freshmen coming in from high school, this is an experience that they had never had before. Again, the whole thing about invisibilization. They didn't even know about it.

I don't know how many of them, even though they must have been dance majors at their high schools, got to Denver ever, to see the Cleo Parker Robinson Company. If you see a video in your class of the Ailey Company, that is so sad of an example of what it means to see a live performance of living dance.
Mm-hmm (affirmative). I do think that's something that ends up being important. I forget that. Literally, people were like, they're in Denver?
Really?
Yeah.
Wow.

Yeah. [crosstalk 00:52:20] Wow.
There's a whole legacy too. There was a time when Cleo was here more often.
Okay.
Back when Mama Tish was here [crosstalk 00:52:32]
Oh yeah. I remember her. Yes.
That was being cultivated.
Yes.
Then Onye comes here.
Yeah.
It's interesting, how it comes in and out.
Yeah, instead of a through line. Now that you're here, maybe you can establish this through line. Yeah.
It's been really exciting to have this with Cleo.
Yes.
To have Millicent come here, and for us to do Bamboo, and to have that exchange with Cleo Parker Robinson and their company, and their 45 years.
Yeah.

Sure.
She had a performance the week before. She has a performance right now.
Yeah.
I was just like, I don't know how, but I really want you to be a part of this.
Uh-huh.
What it requires, I had to go down to Denver and just be there for four hours, do even have a chance to talk to Cleo.
Wow, in all your free time.

In all the free time. It also feels like ... Again, I was going to ask you even more about these African aesthetics, but it feels like also these cultural things that come to be a part of how you have these conversations ...
Yeah.
The conversation at the end, we didn't get quite our full circle on the stage.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).

Just today, Ana and Jaamil and I were just trying to figure out ... It's a little awkward transition to the conversation, but I almost always have post-show discussions. It's not because it's a good check on the box of the grant or the presenter, or whatever. I'm like, I actually want to talk. I'm interested in conversation.
Uh-huh.
I want to know more. I want to hear what you're thinking. I want your questions. I see that with you too, truly interested [crosstalk 00:54:31]

But I'm so different with that, Gesel. When you performed in Philly, we did not do a post-performance conversation. I had a pre-performance session, kind of tangential to what you were doing. I am a purist, who really feels that performance itself is the jewel in the crown. I'm not a big talked, believe it or not. I'm a big writer, maybe.

For me, the most important thing is that people should leave the theater with this feeling of the performance. I hate for it to get dispelled by conversation, because I feel that, then, again, dance gets demonized ... Not exactly demonized, but I can't find the right word, so I'll stay with that. Dance gets demonized, in a way, by being translated into words again.

In one of my lectures, I say, dance is a medium that is illegible in literal translation. How everything then goes back to conversation, or to words, I don't want that privileged with my practice. I want my black dancing body practice and profession to keep the dancing at the center. I feel that, in the last two decades, there's been so much talk, talk, talk.

\That's why it's wonderful that these women ... It's interesting that both of them came out with their things, I'm feeling so alive. That was after a panel, which is really quite interesting. Though the panel had all of these performative elements in it, from all three of us. Michelle, at the end of your performance, I think she was responding to the performance. She wouldn't have had a means of saying that if we hadn't had a conversation, but anyway ... The idea that, to me, the performance is a priory.
I actually do feel similarly. It was interesting in talking to Michelle after that.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
She said something about ... It's so funny, because I kind of said what you ended up saying. She said something about, do we have to have discussion in order for it to feel deep?
Yeah.
I was like, first, let's define deep. There are concerts that I walk out of, and it's enough. I'm feeling.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
I might want to go to the studio. I might want to write.
Yeah.
I might decide I never want to see anything like that again, but I am viscerally in response, instead of just mentally in responses.
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
When I have these conversations, I often also try to derail the mental sort of exercise.
Right.
Trying to use it as another way to stay with the performance.
Mm-hmm (affirmative). Yeah.
I agree that ... Also, valuing. That experience that you're feeling is as important.
Exactly.
As this conversation.
Right.
You don't need the conversation, to have this deep resonance.

Yeah. Again, to not devalue the conversations after, it's all about how they're done. When Helmut and I were performing this piece, Tongues Smell Color, at the end, he and I would facilitate, and we would say, this is not the end of the performance. What we want to do now is offer ... Something like the Bill T. Jones thing that I said. There's another wonderful Bill T. Jones statement, where he said, performance is asking you to look at the performance and se yourself watching it, or something.

We throw out a few things like that and say, what we want you to do is to extend yourself into the performance, by how we engage in conversation. If somebody would say something like, how long did it take you to make this piece? We would say, thank you, and go on to the next. People in our piece, which was totally about race, were coming out with the most incredible things. When you did that walking backwards, and you were saying that thing about growing up in Germany, I remembered that my parents told me about relatives in the Holocaust, or something like that.

There are ways of structuring the conversation, which certainly your panel did. Other ways of structuring the conversation so that you don't take people out. When I do these post-performance things now, I usually start with the people in the audience. I say, I'm working just from images. The thing that I said, the Bill T. Jones thing, about proxy, so that they people who are up there, dancing, are a proxy for your body. That's why you're interested in watching it at all.
I would say, images. Just close your eyes for a minute. Think about the performance. Think about one image. I want you just to speak from one image that you saw. That has been really fruitful. Okay.
I might ask [inaudible 01:00:31] on Sunday, or maybe tonight, we'll see. We were talking about other ways. We were like, what if they had cards?
Mm-hmm (affirmative).
Just so that 10 minute break is not this ...

Again, I don't want to put paper and words in between you and ... I wish it wouldn't have to be ... We did it right after. No, when I did Cynthia Oliver last October, when she came, of course she had to go back and get dressed and come out, I did this image thing, which I think kind of threw her off a little. She wasn't prepared. I was not about to go into a cold Q&A.
